data.title: Oil prices set to climb as Israeli strikes on Iran's energy facilities raise tensions
data.description:
Global oil markets are on high alert following Israeli attacks on Iranian energy infrastructure, which have increased concerns about the stability of crude supplies from the Middle East.

Over the weekend, Israeli forces targeted a natural gas plant linked to Iran’s massive South Pars field, its largest gas reserve, and also hit fuel storage sites, in what appears to be a calculated move against Iran’s domestic energy systems, according to multiple news reports.

While the strikes did not directly affect Iran’s oil exports, the disruption has fueled anxieties among traders and analysts, especially after oil prices saw their biggest single-day spike in three years last Friday.

Iran, despite facing U.S. sanctions, remains the third-largest producer within OPEC. Its regional allies, particularly the Houthi group in Yemen, have a history of attacking commercial vessels, and Tehran has long threatened, though never acted on, closing the Strait of Hormuz, a vital chokepoint for global oil shipments.

Analysts anticipate that tensions in the region will push crude prices higher in the near term. Bob McNally, president of Rapidan Energy and former energy adviser at the White House, was quoted by Bloomberg News as saying the widening conflict, especially if it continues to target infrastructure and results in civilian harm, will likely add a "risk premium" to oil markets as the week begins.

On Friday, U.S. benchmark crude (West Texas Intermediate) (CL1:COM) briefly surged 14% before closing near $73 a barrel. JPMorgan has estimated that if the Strait of Hormuz were to be shut down, international oil prices could jump to $130 a barrel, an increase that would add pressure to already elevated global inflation levels.

Reports from Iranian media indicated that Saturday’s airstrikes caused a large explosion and fire at the Phase 14 gas processing plant and shut down a production platform at South Pars.

If Iranian oil supplies were significantly disrupted, the U.S. could urge OPEC+, the alliance led by Saudi Arabia and Russia, to use spare production capacity. Helima Croft of RBC Capital Markets, a former CIA analyst, pointed out that while this is a possible contingency, it’s uncertain whether OPEC could fully compensate for a prolonged outage in Iran’s output, which averages around 3.4 million barrels per day, Bloomberg News reported.

Deploying spare capacity from Saudi Arabia or the UAE could also carry political risks. During the Trump administration’s earlier pressure campaign on Iran, a retaliatory drone strike by the Houthis in 2019 disabled key Saudi oil infrastructure at Abqaiq.

The International Energy Agency (IEA), which monitors global energy supplies, said current fuel inventories remain healthy, aided by recent OPEC+ output increases and slowing demand growth. The agency also said it stands ready to release emergency reserves if needed.

Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump, posting on Truth Social, called for both sides to reach a peace agreement. He had previously expressed frustration over rising energy prices even before the Israeli strikes occurred.

data.title: 2nd death reported in Sarepta gene therapy trial for Duchenne muscular dystrophy
data.description:
Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT) reported a second fatality from acute liver failure in a patient treated with its gene therapy, Elevidys, designed for Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

The death follows a similar incident three months ago, in which a teenage boy died after receiving the same therapy. Both individuals were non-ambulatory, or  unable to walk because of advanced stages of the disease.

As a result, Sarepta (NASDAQ:SRPT) has halted an ongoing clinical trial and temporarily suspended distribution of Elevidys for patients who are no longer able to walk. The company is also evaluating whether stronger immune-suppressing treatments might be needed for this group of patients.

In a statement, Sarepta’s Chief Scientific Officer Louise Rodino-Klapac emphasized that patient safety remains the company’s top concern, Bloomberg News reported.

FDA may face scrutiny

This latest incident casts a shadow over Elevidys, Sarepta’s flagship therapy, and may increase scrutiny of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s decision to approve the drug.

Elevidys was initially authorized under the FDA’s accelerated approval pathway, which allows therapies to reach the market before definitive clinical benefits have been demonstrated. At the time of approval, clinical studies hadn’t shown a measurable slowing of disease progression, but the drug had been observed to increase dystrophin levels, the protein lacking in individuals with DMD.

Despite inconclusive results from several trials, Elevidys received full approval from the FDA last year for ambulatory patients. For non-ambulatory patients, the drug was granted an additional, conditional approval. The gene therapy, priced at $3.2 million a dose, remains the most important product in Sarepta’s (SRPT) portfolio.

Duchenne muscular dystrophy affects approximately 15,000 people in the United States, primarily boys. The genetic disorder disrupts production of dystrophin, a protein essential for muscle function.

The condition leads to progressive muscle degeneration, often forcing patients into wheelchairs during adolescence. Many die in early adulthood, although some have seen extended life expectancy thanks to evolving treatments.

The approval of Elevidys sparked controversy within the FDA. The agency’s leadership overruled internal objections to greenlight the therapy. Among the critics was Dr. Vinay Prasad, now head of the FDA’s gene therapy division, who has publicly questioned the decision.

In March, he posted on social media that the treatment appeared to be harming children with DMD, citing fatal liver complications, Bloomberg News reported.

data.title: Biggest weekly communication services gainers and losers: Walt Disney leads, T-Mobile top decliner
data.description:
Wall Street closed the week lower amid U.S.-China trade developments and rising geopolitical tensions. President Trump announced a trade framework with China on rare earths and student access, while Israel's strikes on Iranian targets spiked oil prices and boosted energy stocks.

Overall, the sector, represented by the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLC), fell 1.4% in the week, while the broader market (S&P 500) lost 0.4%.

Weekly top gainers

  • The Walt Disney Company (DIS) +3.6% Walt Disney has closed its deal to fully acquire Comcast's (CMCSA) stake in Hulu, providing the Mouse House with 100% ownership in the streaming entity.
  • Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) +2.1% Warner Bros. Discovery confirmed it will split into two companies — one focused on streaming and the other focused on its networks.
  • Comcast (CMCSA) +0.9% Disney is paying $438.7M to Comcast to acquire the remaining stake in Hulu. 
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) +0.6% and (GOOG) +0.6%
  • AT&T (T) +0.4% AT&T plans to repurchase at least $3B in shares by year-end and reiterated full-year 2025 financial and operational guidance.

Weekly top losers

  • T-Mobile US (TMUS) -7.3%
  • Match Group (MTCH) -5.2%
  • Live Nation Entertainment (LYV) -4.2% Live Nation will invest $1B to build out 18 new venues in smaller U.S. cities.
  • Omnicom Group (OMC) -2.7%  The FTC under the Trump administration may approve the Omnicom-Interpublic merger, provided the combined company doesn't boycott platforms over political content by withholding client ads.
  • Netflix (NFLX) -2.4%
 

ETFs to tab the communication services sector include: (NYSEARCA:XLC), (VOX), (IYZ), (RSPC), and (XTL).

data.title: Airbus is said to revisit bigger A220 model as Paris Air Show nears
data.description:
Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) (OTCPK:EADSF) is once again evaluating the prospect of expanding its A220 lineup with a stretched version of the A220-300, informally dubbed the A220-500, as it seeks to boost sales momentum and attract new airline customers, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

The initiative comes amid the opening of the Paris Air Show, where the European manufacturer is also hoping to secure fresh orders for its current A220, including a potential deal with AirAsia for up to 100 jets, a source familiar with the discussions said to the news outlet.

The proposed A220-500 would offer increased seating capacity but sacrifice some range, making it less suitable for long-haul routes such as coast-to-coast flights in the United States. However, it would provide a more contemporary alternative to the smaller variants of Airbus’s (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) A320 family and could challenge Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) 737 Max in the lower end of the narrowbody market, where Boeing (NYSE:BA) currently lacks a direct counter.

Commercial hurdles

Despite its technical appeal, the A220 program faces commercial hurdles. The aircraft backlog has declined, and the program remains unprofitable. Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) recorded a net reduction of 10 orders since early 2024, and the A220 has not secured any new purchases this year, instead losing one through cancellation. As of the end of May, the backlog hovered just below 500 units.

Sources cited by Bloomberg News said Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) is leaning toward a low-complexity upgrade that retains the existing wing and engine setup. This would streamline development and reduce certification risks, while avoiding costly design changes. Although it would restrict range capabilities, the approach is seen as a practical solution that could be implemented more quickly and economically.

However, no final decision has been made, and the timeline remains uncertain. An Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) spokesperson said the company continues to invest in the A220 family and is reviewing all options, emphasizing that decisions on future variants are not made until fully confirmed.

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) inherited design studies for a third A220 model when it took over the program from Bombardier (OTCQX:BDRBF) (OTCQX:BDRAF) (OTCPK:BOMBF) in 2018. Originally launched as the C-Series, the aircraft struggled to gain commercial traction under Bombardier’s ownership.

Narrowbody marketplace

The potential A220-500 would fill a gap in the narrowbody segment, where demand for next-generation aircraft continues to outpace supply. Both Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) and Boeing (BA) have largely sold out their A320 and 737 lines into the 2030s, and neither is close to launching a clean-sheet successor.

The longer A220 would likely encounter fewer regulatory obstacles due to design commonality with the A220-300. This configuration may appeal to carriers like Air France-KLM, which operate shorter-haul routes and are less concerned about transcontinental range.

U.S. airlines, on the other hand, might view the limited range as a drawback, given the profitability of long domestic flights. Nevertheless, Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) could benefit from avoiding a new engine program with Pratt & Whitney (NYSE:RTX) at a time when the engine maker is already dealing with an expensive maintenance crisis involving its GTF line, which has grounded hundreds of jets. A more powerful engine would necessitate significant redesigns of the wing and pylon structures, potentially adding billions in development costs.

data.title: ‘How to Train Your Dragon’ roars to No. 1 at box office with $83.7M debut
data.description:
DreamWorks' (NASDAQ:CMCSA) "How to Train Your Dragon" took flight at the domestic box office this weekend, delivering a better-than-forecast $83.7 million opening and easily claiming the top spot in North America, according to studio estimates from Comscore.

The animated adventure from the NBCUniversal (NASDAQ:CMCSA) unit opened in 4,356 theaters, generating a strong $19,215 per-location average and dominated the competition amid a packed field of summer releases. Its debut accounts for more than half of the weekend’s $146.5 million total from the top ten films.

Disney’s (NYSE:DIS) "Lilo & Stitch" remake, now in its fourth week, held on to second place with $15.5 million, bringing its cumulative total to $366.4 million, the highest among current releases. The film dropped 52% from the previous weekend as it began to lose momentum.

A24’s new adult drama "Materialists" opened solidly in third place, bringing in $12 million across 2,844 locations. Despite its more modest scale, it posted a strong per-theater average of $4,220, suggesting strong interest from arthouse and upscale audiences.

Rounding out the top five were "Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning" (NASDAQ:PARA) and "Ballerina", earning $10.3 million and $9.4 million, respectively, in their fourth and second weekends. Tom Cruise’s latest stunt-filled entry has now grossed $166.3 million domestically.

Meanwhile, several titles experienced significant drop-offs. Sony’s "Karate Kid: Legends" (NYSE:SONY) fell 42% to $5 million in its third weekend, while Warner Bros.' (NASDAQ:WBD) "Final Destination: Bloodlines" dipped 40%, earning $3.9 million in its fifth frame.

On the indie front, the thriller "Life of Chuck" expanded to over 1,000 screens and surged 855% from last week, taking in $2.1 million — a modest but noteworthy rise. However, Warner Bros.’ (NASDAQ:WBD) long-running "Sinners" crawled to a quiet ninth weekend finish, earning just $1.47 million.

Despite a few underperformers, the weekend box office showed signs of renewed strength heading into the heart of the summer movie season. With tentpoles like "Dragon" delivering franchise-sized numbers, studios will be hoping to sustain the momentum as more high-profile releases roll out in the coming weeks, according to Comscore.

Top 10 box office for the weekend of June 13–15 in the United States and Canada, along with their estimated weekend grosses:

  1. How to Train Your Dragon (Universal) – $83,700,000
  2. Lilo & Stitch (Disney) – $15,500,000
  3. Materialists (A24) – $12,000,423
  4. Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning (Paramount) – $10,300,000
  5. Ballerina (Lionsgate) – $9,400,000
  6. Karate Kid: Legends (Sony) – $5,000,000
  7. Final Destination: Bloodlines (Warner Bros.) – $3,900,000
  8. The Phoenician Scheme (Focus Features) – $3,050,000
  9. The Life of Chuck ((Neon)) – $2,144,250
  10. Sinners (Warner Bros.) – $1,470,000

Top 10 Weekend Total: $146,464,673

data.title: Top weekly consumer staples gainers/losers: Archer-Daniels-Midland tops, J. M. Smucker bottoms
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages on Friday plumbed fresh session lows, after Israel's military said Iran had fired projectiles against the country. Amidst these geopolitical tensions, a renewed tariff threat by U.S. President Donald Trump continued to alarm traders.

Due to these events, the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) for the week, which holds a 6.31% weightage on the S&P 500, saw a marginal decline in price of 0.75%, where the S&P 500's (SP500) was also marginally 0.37% lower. However, the sector's year-to-date performance is considerably higher, +2.99%, which is notably below the benchmark S&P 500's (SP500) 1.62%.

Top gainers this week:

  • Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) +9.7% as upgraded to Buy from a previous investment rating of Neutral by analysts at financial-services firm UBS. Analyst Manav Gupta highlighted that proposed changes in U.S. biofuel policy could significantly boost demand for domestic soybean oil, a key profit driver for ADM.
  • Bunge Global (BG) +9.2% as it received regulatory approval from China for its acquisition of grain handler Viterra, removing the last major hurdle to the deal's conclusion, which is now expected by July 2.
  • The Hershey (HSY) +4.6% as it is seeking a cocoa exemption from tariffs as it is not grown in the US.
  • Philip Morris International (PM) ended the week 1.4% higher.
  • Mondelez International (MDLZ) was also 1.2% higher for the week.

Top losers this week:

  • The J. M. Smucker (SJM) slides 13.7% after the food company set its guidance below expectations. The headwinds from tariffs, reduced SBS profitability, increased marketing, and assumed coffee elasticity lead, to a wide-ranging, and below consensus FY26 margin/profit outlook.
  • Brown-Forman (BF.B) -5.9% as it outlines low single-digit organic net sales decline for fiscal 2026 amid volatile global spirits market.
  • The Campbell's (CPB) traded 4.9% lower for the week as it signals low-end 2025 EPS guidance amid snacks softness and tariff headwinds.
  • Constellation Brands (STZ) -4.1% as beverage sales were sluggish during May. The soft data supports a persisting theme of broad slowing in U.S. consumer staples demand that has been seen over the last few months.
  • Conagra Brands (CAG) -3.7% as it faces a “meaningful” headwind to earnings growth along with increased margin pressures, leading BofA Securities to downgrade the packaged food producer to Underperform from Neutral, and shave 26% off its price target.

data.title: Trump is said to have opposed Israeli plan to target Iran’s supreme leader
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump recently rejected a proposal by Israel to assassinate Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Reuters reported Sunday, citing two U.S. officials.

A senior U.S. administration official said the United States wouldn’t support targeting Iranian political leadership unless American lives had been lost.

The officials, who spoke under the condition of anonymity, indicated that high-level communication between Washington and Jerusalem has been ongoing following Israel’s large-scale offensive aimed at disrupting Iran’s nuclear capabilities.

Israeli leaders informed U.S. counterparts that they had a window to eliminate Khamenei, but Trump declined to support the move, Reuters reported. It remains unclear whether the directive came directly from Trump or through intermediaries, though he has been in frequent contact with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

In a Sunday interview on Fox News, Netanyahu dismissed the Reuters report, saying, “There are so many false reports of conversations that never happened, and I’m not going to get into that.” He added, “We’ll do what we need to do. And I think the United States knows what is good for the United States.”

Trump, who has shown continued interest in reopening negotiations with Tehran over its nuclear ambitions, acknowledged in a Friday interview with Reuters that his team was fully informed about Israel’s military actions. Talks scheduled to take place in Oman were reportedly scrapped in the wake of the attacks.

data.title: S&P 500 looks expensive, but for good reason, BofA's Subramanian says
data.description:
The Standard & Poor’s 500 (SP500) stock index is statistically expensive on every valuation metric tracked by Bank of America, but equity and quant strategist Savita Subramanian argues this is a feature, not a bug, of today's high-quality, tech-heavy index.

In a June 12 report to clients, Subramanian said the S&P 500 (SP500) trades at 21 times forward earnings, about 35% above its historical average, and looks overpriced across all 20 valuation metrics BofA monitors. Yet she cautions against historical comparisons that ignore how dramatically the index has evolved.

Nearly 70% of the S&P in 1980 was made up of asset-intensive manufacturing sectors, the report said, adding that today the figure is less than 20%. The valuation conversation is a comparison of “apples and oranges,” Subramanian said.

Today’s index boasts higher quality, lower leverage, reduced earnings volatility and stronger margins. These structural improvements make mean reversion to historical valuation norms a misleading expectation, she argues.

U.S. premium to global markets is justified

Subramanian also defends the approximately 40% valuation premium the U.S. commands over Europe and Asia, pointing to superior balance sheets, corporate transparency, and long-term growth potential.

According to BofA’s research:

  • The U.S. is half as levered as other global regions
  • Earnings volatility is lower than in Europe
  • Free cash flow per share is higher than both Asia and Europe
  • The percentage of non-earning companies is lower
  • Forecast dispersion is tighter, reflecting greater transparency

Add in structural advantages such as the U.S. dollar’s reserve currency status, energy independence and dominance in technology, and Subramanian sees little reason for the valuation gap to close.

Sector strategy: Positioning for summer

BofA’s tactical model suggests investors lean into Communication Services, Utilities and Technology within the U.S., identifying Interactive Media & Services, Metals & Mining and Independent Power & Renewable Electricity Producers as top “opportunity” industries.

Outside the U.S., Subramanian recommends being selective:

  • Favor U.S. Utilities over Asia Utilities
  • Choose Asia Communication Services over European peers
  • Consider European Consumer Discretionary over U.S. names

Meanwhile, Consumer Staples Distribution & Retail stands out as a potential “momentum blowup,” a warning for investors chasing recent winners without underlying support.

data.title: Top weekly S&P 500 consumer discretionary gainers & losers: Tesla takes lead; Lululemon bottoms
data.description:
For the week ending June 13, the S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary sector (NYSEARCA:XLY) saw a decline in price of 1.00%, underperforming the broader S&P 500's (SP500) 0.48% decline. However, the sector's year-to-date performance is even weaker, down 5.62%, significantly trailing the benchmark's 1.62% rise.

Global markets faced a fresh wave of uncertainty this week, impacting consumer discretionary stocks, after Israel's military strike on Iran escalated Middle East tensions. This added pressure to markets already strained by President Trump's aggressive and unpredictable trade policies.

Top S&P 500 consumer discretionary gainers:

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) +10.22% as President Trump and CEO Elon Musk mend their relationship. Speaking at the White House earlier on Thursday, Trump called Musk “a friend” and has reopened dialogue with the Tesla CEO after a very public feud last week.

Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) +4.04% It is reducing prices on some iced drinks in China to stay competitive amid cautious consumers. CEO Brian Niccol noted strong interest in selling a stake in its China business. The company is also testing a new protein cold foam at select U. S. locations to improve its menu.

General Motors’ (NYSE:GM) +2.49% due to plans for a plug-in hybrid, increased U.S. investments, and an optimistic Q2 earnings forecast, according to GM's chief financial officer Paul Jacobson at the Deutsche Bank Global Auto Industry Conference.

Hasbro (NASDAQ:HAS) +2.25%

D.R. Horton (NYSE:DHI) +1.94%

Top S&P 500 consumer discretionary losers:

lululemon athletica (NASDAQ:LULU) -9.86% after Barclays reduced LULU's price target to $270 from $276, maintaining an Equal Weight rating. The company's Q1 results met expectations, but same-store sales growth fell short of estimates due to traffic issues, the analyst tells investors in a research note.

Norwegian Cruise Line (NYSE:NCLH) -9.10%

Ross Stores (NASDAQ:ROST) -8.31%

Carnival Corporation (CCL) -7.70%

Deckers Outdoor (NYSE:DECK) -7.09%

Notable Consumer Discretionary ETFs include: (NYSEARCA:XLY), (NYSEARCA:VCR), (NYSEARCA:FXD), (NYSEARCA:FDIS), (NYSEARCA:RSPD), and (NYSEARCA:RXI).

data.title: Oil spike amplifies the current stagflation shock - Apollo
data.description:
The jump in oil prices following Israel's strikes on Iran could exacerbate U.S. stagflationary conditions if they persist, according to Apollo Global Management.

Oil prices (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) (NYSEARCA:USO) (BNO) rallied more than 7% in Friday's session, the biggest one-day rise in three years.

"According to the Fed’s model of the US economy, a sustained $10 increase in oil prices is expected to increase inflation by 0.4% (including secondary effects) and lower GDP by 0.4%," Chief Economist Torsten Slock said.

WTI is up more than $10 from its lows at the end of May.

"Tariffs also increase inflation and lower GDP growth," he said. "Restrictions on immigration also increase wage inflation and lower employment growth."

"In short, higher oil prices exacerbate the ongoing stagflation shock stemming from tariffs and immigration restrictions."

"Stagflation is a problem for the FOMC when they meet," Slock added. "Higher inflation says the Fed should be hiking. Lower GDP growth says the Fed should cutting. So will the FOMC next week put more weight on the upward pressure on inflation or more weight on the coming slowdown in growth?"
